SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims (that stacked Bi-RNN and Grid-RNN models without syntactic features outperform prior baselines on NAIST Text Corpus 1.5) are supported by experiments including bootstrap significance testing (Sec. 6.2). Mathematical model descriptions (stacked Bi-RNN, Grid-RNN, GRU as g, softmax output) are consistent and no obvious theoretical errors are present. Some implementation and hyperparameter details (exact embedding sizes, training epochs, full Eq.1/2 definitions) are omitted in the provided text, which limits full reproducibility but does not undermine the main empirical claims.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies Grid-RNNs to model multi-predicate interactions for Japanese dependency-based PAS, which is a novel combination in this specific task setting (Sec. 4). Prior work used joint models relying on syntactic features or different neural architectures (Sec. 5.1, 5.3), and the authors clearly differentiate their contribution as syntax-free, sentence-level neural modeling of multi-predicate interactions. The technique is a non-trivial adaptation of existing RNN/Grid ideas rather than a purely incremental application.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized into standard sections (task, models, experiments) and defines core components (Input/RNN/Grid/Output layers, GRU, residual connections), with equations for the recurrent updates (Sec. 3.2, 3.3, 4.1–4.3). However, the provided text contains repeated and fragmented passages and several referenced figures/tables are missing from the excerpt, making parts harder to follow and leaving some notation (Eq.1/2 mapping) underspecified in this version.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Experiments use a standard dataset (NAIST 1.5) with appropriate baselines (Imamura et al. 2009, Ouchi et al. 2015), significance testing, and analyses of depth/residual effects and per-case-role performance (Sec. 6.1–6.2). Still, the paper lacks detailed ablations (e.g., input feature variants), comprehensive hyperparameter settings, error analysis or discussion of limitations in the provided text, which would strengthen reproducibility and diagnostic insight.

IMPACT.justification: The work tackles an important and challenging problem in pro-drop languages—zero anaphora in Japanese PAS—and demonstrates improved performance without syntactic parsers, which can reduce error propagation in pipeline systems (Introduction, Sec. 6.2). These results are likely to influence researchers building end-to-end or syntax-agnostic PAS/SRL systems and open avenues for multi-predicate neural modeling in other languages.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (neural modeling for predicate-argument structure analysis) and methods (RNN/Grid-RNN, residual connections) are squarely within the scope of top-tier NLP/ML conferences. The paper uses standard datasets, baselines, and evaluation metrics expected by the community (NAIST Text Corpus 1.5, F-measure, bootstrap testing), and is positioned with relevant prior work (Sec. 5).

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Comparisons are made against strong, relevant baselines including the state-of-the-art joint model of Ouchi et al. (2015) on the same NAIST 1.5 splits, and significance testing is reported (Sec. 6.2). The paper acknowledges incomparability with some prior neural studies that used unpublished datasets, but could include additional ablations or comparisons to other neural architectures (e.g., CRF-based sequence models) to be fully comprehensive.
